Ch3.title: Short-term health and care insurance products Syllabus objectives 1.1 Understand in detail and analyse the major health and care insurance products offered: 1.1.1 The benefits and other main features of the following products: health cash plans major medical expenses private medical insurance group and individual covers. 1.1.2 Key features of the health and care insurance products outlined in 1.1.1: customer needs.

content.text: Short-term health and care insurance products Syllabus objectives 1.1 Understand in detail and analyse the major health and care insurance products offered: 1.1.1 The benefits and other main features of the following products: health cash plans major medical expenses private medical insurance group and individual covers. 1.1.2 Key features of the health and care insurance products outlined in 1.1.1: customer needs.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists. [src:Notes]
(This is only* part *of Syllabus objective* 1.1) Private medical insurance
1.1 Benefits and features

[...] Private medical insurance (PMI) aims to indemnify the policyholder against the costs incurred through medical treatment. The level of cover is likely to be dependent on the State benefits available.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

In countries where there is no State funded healthcare, before private medical insurance (PMI) products were available, individuals paid for their own medical costs under a 'fee-for-service' model. PMI products were developed to reduce the risk to individuals of having to pay for their own costs. The State may or may not make PMI compulsory.

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists. [src:Notes]

Where there is a comprehensive national health service such as the National Health Service (NHS) in the UK, insurance initially had a role to play in providing the funds for a policyholder to choose the how, where and when of medical care delivery to suit their requirements over and above what was available on the NHS. PMI was also seen as being complementary to the NHS, covering acute, treatable conditions rather than chronic conditions or accident and emergency treatment.
Insurance could also be viewed as competing with the national health service.
Question
Outline the ways in which PMI could be considered to be competing with a national healthcare service?
Solution

Private medical care will be competing with the State system for resources, including doctors, nurses and potentially services such as scans, hospital beds and medical tests. Whilst the use of PMI may 'free-up' space within the State healthcare system, the individuals providing care under PMI are often the same as those providing it under the State system. So, these individuals potentially spend more time dealing with patients under the private system and therefore have less availability to treat patients under the State system.
This is considered further in Chapter 4.

There may be exclusions on what is covered under PMI. For example, the following are typically excluded from cover: Drug abuse, self-inflicted injuries, out-patient drugs and dressings, normal pregnancy, cosmetic surgery, gender reassignment (also known as sex change), preventative treatment, kidney dialysis, mobility aids, experimental treatment, experimental drugs, organ treatment, war risks and injuries arising from dangerous hobbies (often called hazardous pursuits).

Exclusions are more likely where PMI is being provided as a supplement to a State system. Where there is no State system, exclusions to cover are much harder to impose. However, there are likely to still be restrictions on what is covered. For example, cosmetic surgery or weight-loss procedures may still be prohibited.

Where exclusions do apply, proving that a particular illness or injury falls under the exclusions of a policy can be difficult. For example, if an individual participated in rock climbing as a hobby, injuries incurred through this activity may be excluded from cover. If the individual then fell and broke their ankle whilst climbing, it could be difficult to prove that this occurred whilst carrying out an excluded activity and was not just an accident in the home, for example.
The cost of any claim may be paid directly to the care provider or to the patient if they have paid the provider.

What PMI may pay for varies. Where there is no State healthcare, or PMI is compulsory, PMI may just pay for medical care. If an individual needed to stay in hospital overnight, this could incur additional accommodation costs. Where there is a comprehensive State healthcare system, it is more likely that PMI will pay for all costs incurred (perhaps up to an upper limit) including accommodation costs PMI is generally annually renewable insurance. Premiums can be rated by factors such as age, gender (if permitted by local regulations) and previous medical history (if permitted by local regulations). Where cover is compulsory, any rating permitted may be limited. In particular, previous medical history may not be allowed to be considered. This is to prevent individuals or groups becoming 'uninsurable'.
60-month moratorium (not 48); PED waiting period max 36 months; 1-hr/3-hr cashless TATs; 30-day claim settlement mandate; Bima Sugam digital marketplace launch. [src:Notes]
For example, in Switzerland, health insurance is compulsory. Insurers are not permitted to rate the basic health insurance product by age or medical condition.
1.2 Insurer's perspective

Claims The indemnity nature of PMI leads to issues around very large claim amounts being incurred. Including limits within the terms of a policy can manage this, however, this may mean the policy does not indemnify the policyholder.
Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

Generally, increasing costs of claims can also be an issue for PMI providers. Arrangements. with healthcare providers (eg health maintenance organisations (HMOs) or preferred provider organisations (PPOs)) can help manage the cost. This potentially restricts policyholder choice around care provision.

An HMO is a group of care providers (including hospitals and doctors). A policy that restricts cover to HMOs will only allow care to be received from those who work within its organisation, Similarly, PPOs are lists of providers that the insurer prefers care to be provided by. The insurer may have agreed preferential rates with these providers and so their use reduces costs for both the insurer and the insured.

Claim rates may be higher than anticipated. The exclusion of pre-existing conditions and the use of underwriting can help manage the element of this that is due to anti-selection. Where pre-existing condition exclusions/underwriting are prohibited (eg because PMI is compulsory) the insurer will need to ensure it covers a range of lives and the mix accepted is the same as that assumed in the pricing basis. GP referrals can also be used as a gatekeeper for claims*.* The point being made here about covering a range of lives is important. In the USA, the Affordable Care Act (ACA) introduced financial penalties (called an 'individual mandate') for not taking out medical insurance. In 2016/2017 this was the greater of 2.5% of household income or $695 per adult ($347.50 per child) to a maximum of $2,085. The aim was to try and ensure that everyone (including the younger, healthier lives) took out healthcare insurance to try and reduce the risk overall. These penalties were effectively abolished in 2019 (by setting the penalty to $0). Some States have introduced local penalties to ensure the whole population has medical insurance Excesses and/or coinsurance (deductibles) can also be applied to reduce the likelihood of small claims being made. No claims discounts could also be used to reward those with fewer claims.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

These can also be used as ways to drive down insurance premiums, particularly in countries with very high costs of medical treatments. For example, policies could have coinsurance of 20% where the insurer pays 80% of costs and the insured 20%. However, this may only start once the individual has paid for, for example, the first $3,000 of treatment in a given policy year. Again, in the USA, individual out-of-pocket payments was something the ACA tried to limit by imposing maximum limits in a year before the insurer would cover 100% of bills (in 2016 this was $6,850 for an individual). However, this only applied to policies purchased through the ACA marketplace and employer-sponsored plans could still have had different limits.

More generally, insurers may try and encourage policyholders to lead 'healthy' lives by rewarding activity (eg using pedometers to track activity levels) or healthy choices. (eg monitoring food purchased via links with supermarkets). Rewards could be paid as cash, retail discounts or discounted future premiums.

Over time, the types of claims made under PMI may change. For example, as diagnostic tests become more readily available for certain conditions, policyholders may expect these tests to be covered by their PMI. This may in turn lead to further medical tests or treatment.

Underwriting Underwriting is a key area for PMI. The level of underwriting permitted may be determined by local legislation. Where there is no such legislation, underwriting will need to be consistent with other local insurers in order to reduce the risk of anti-selection. All proposal forms need to be clear and comprehensive to reduce the risk of non-disclosure and future disputed claims. However, an onerous application process may deter sales and so a balance needs to be struck.

OFF 2024 Examinations Fages 1.3 Meeting customer needs Where the State does provide cover, insurance may be required or desired in order to access faster treatment, a particular hospital/consultant or perceived higher quality service such as private accommodation during in-patient treatment.

For example, in the UK, the NHS concentrates on the provision of childcare, accident and emergency cover, maternity care and treatment for chronic life-threatening diseases such as cancer. However, for non-emergency and less serious treatments (such as hip operations, cataract or varicose vein removal) patients may have to wait many months to see a consultant and longer still to receive treatment. Beds in NHS hospitals are often set out in wards with little privacy.

There may also be a perception that private hospitals are cleaner than NHS ones, and so there is less risk of contracting a hospital-acquired infection such as MRSA PMI can provide indemnity for private medical treatment and thus address these needs.

Some PMI plans in the UK have specifically addressed the need for faster care by only providing cover for treatments where the waiting list is longer than a certain period (eg 6-week plans). The price of these policies is lower than more comprehensive plans.

Having PMI increases choice by allowing patients access to non-NHS hospitals and specialists working privately, but most PMI policies restrict this choice by allowing treatment only in hospitals within certain price bands. Again, this is to reduce costs and yet still give the level of cover required. The greatest choice will be available for those paying the highest premiums. This choice will include the most expensive hospitals (in London and the South East, and hospitals that are 'centres of excellence' in particular fields).
Many private consultants also work in the NHS. There are restrictions on the amount of private work consultants with an NHS contract can undertake.
Question
Suggest why, if you can see the same consultant in the State healthcare system for free, you would want or need to see a consultant privately.
Solution

It may take a long time to see a consultant in the State system, as there may be a lengthy waiting list. If you are ill, you will want to find out what is wrong with you quickly. This is not only for peace of mind, but also so that you can be treated quicker.

It may be that you wish to see a consultant without having to involve your own doctor. Note that, to cover consultation fees, PMI policies will usually require a referral from your own GP. If you chose this alternative route you would have to pay the consultant's fee yourself, as it would not be covered by insurance.
It may make it easier for you to see the consultant you want as the choice of consultants to whom your GP can refer you may be limited.

IFF 2024 Examinations SAL01 Short term health and care insurance products Consultants working privately (and being paid for it) may give more time to each patient and thus may be able to give more in-depth treatment and, arguably, better diagnosis. You may have a perception that private consultants are better.

If the State does not provide cover, insurance may be compulsory in order to access treatment or avoid incurring high levels of personal debt. If the State does not provide cover for a certain treatment, eg expensive drugs, or if this cover is not available, insurance can provide funding for private treatment.

Shortfalls in State healthcare provision could therefore encourage consumers to seek alternatives. For example: company bosses could seek to have their key staff covered, not just as a perk of executive position, but also to ensure that persons vital to the enterprise were treated quickly with minimal disruption to the work effort. individuals could take out insurance in their own right, for private rooms as well as for the avoidance of waiting for treatment from the State healthcare system.
Thus products therefore tend to evolve to meet a customer's needs.
Waiting list plans are an example of an evolution of PMI to meet a specific customer need.

Certain individuals (or employers on behalf of employees) seek treatment for illnesses or conditions in private hospitals where provision of care can be attained swiftly and in relative comfort. Insurance is an obvious way of spreading the cost in advance of need over all such customers who seek the security of knowing that the expense will be met when required. © 2024 Examinations Health cash plans 2.1 Benefits and features SAL- Short-term health and care insurance products Health cash plans are annually renewable, defined benefit products available primarily within the UK market. The benefits are generally paid as cash direct to the policyholder.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]
Benefits are paid for a range of healthcare related events including: dental optical physiotherapy maternity hospitalisation recuperation hearing aids consultation.

Cover is typically purchased in 'units' and purchasing additional units leads to additional premiums being payable. A waiting period may apply before any claims can be made on the policy (which may exclude treatment following accidents). For any maternity (or new child) benefit a waiting period of twelve months typically applies.
Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918] [src:IAI_Synthesis]
Premiums are generally community rated and low given the fixed benefits payable.
2.2 Insurer's perspective
Claim size The fixed benefits offered on heath cash plans reduce the risk of very large claims that exists on indemnity type products.
Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

There may be more claims than expected on a particular product. Waiting periods and exclusions for pre-existing conditions can be used to manage this risk. No claims discounts could also be used to discourage smaller claims. There may also be a limit on the number of treatments that are paid for in a period of insurance. This will be particularly important where no GP referral is required before a claim is made.

There is the potential that a claim paid could be for a higher amount than the costs the policyholder has incurred. A limit on the total amount payable may therefore be applied (for example, limiting any claim to 50% of the medical bill). This can also be applied as coinsurance.

Premium The low level of premium income generated from health cash plans means that in order to recover the initial costs incurred, the insurer is dependent on policy renewals to generate a profit. However, costs of underwriting and claim management will be significantly lower than PMI insurance.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

CIFF 2024 Examinations The Actuanal Education Company SALO Short-term heath and care insurance products 2.3 Meeting customer needs Where full PMI is too expensive, a health cash plan can help individuals get private treatment from certain specialists (eg physiotherapists, dentists) without incurring the full cost themselves. It is not intended to cover larger procedures such as operations.

As such, health cash plans would generally be preferred by those with lower levels of income Where customers have PMI cover, there may still be a need for a health cash plan. PMI does not typically include cover for areas such as optical or dental care. A health cash plan may offer a fixed amount for a night's stay in a hospital, and this may be payable whether it is a private or State hospital. This could be used to cover additional costs that may be incurred through the hospital stay eg paying for transport to the hospital or additional childcare.

Some PMI policies offer this type of cash payment for an overnight stay in hospital as one of the standard benefits. 3.1 Major medical expense insurance Benefits and features The Glossary defines two different versions of major medical expenses contracts - one specific to the UK and the other specific to the USA In this section, we will be looking at the USA-specific- definition MME in the USA is a comprehensive PMI product type offering reimbursement for the costs of primary, secondary and tertiary care as defined in the policy As you will see, there are many similarities between MME insurance and a comprehensive PMI product. However, the focus for the product is to cap exposure to very large medical costs, rather than cover all medical costs on an indemnity basis.

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists. [src:Notes]

Major medical expense insurance was developed to compensate individuals for particularly large medical expenses due to a severe or prolonged illness, usually by the insurer paying a high percentage of the individual's medical bills.
It was introduced in the United States in the 1950s, to reflect the fact that medical costs were becoming more significant than they had been previously.

Major medical expense insurance differed from earlier forms of medical expense insurance available in the USA as for the first time it covered the different services of health care costs (ie hospital, doctor and ancillary costs) within one common policy.
The list of health care expenses that are covered under the policy (which can be referred to as covered services or covered expenses) are normally set out in the policy conditions.
The precise services covered may vary by insurer, but most major medical expense policies include coverage for a wide range of services and procedures.

The policy will also often list the type of services that are not covered by the insurance policy. In some States in the US, the local State regulator may require a certain minimum combination of covered services if the policy is to be called a 'major medical insurance' policy.

Once covered services are defined for a policy, the policy will also define how benefits are calculated for the covered services. It is common in the USA that the insured is still liable for a share of the cost of benefits, because it provides the insured with a financial incentive to keep costs down. The portion of costs allocated to the insured is sometimes referred to as 'cost sharing'.

This is known as 'coinsurance This is the key difference between major medical insurance and PMI. As major medical insurance is intended to cover large costs, it still requires a potentially significant contribution from policyholders. However, if a claim was for $100,000, with a 10% coinsurance arrangement, it would save the policyholder $90,000.

A deductible amount may be specified in the policy conditions. That is, the insured must meet the first amount of any costs, before any benefits are payable by the insurer. The deductible can apply to all services under the contract.

This is also known as an 'excess The Achvatal Education Company 3.2 SAL-04 Short-term health and care insurance producti It is common that once the deductible has been met, the insurer only pays a percentage of the claim cost above the deductible.

So, it is possible to have a MME insurance policy with both deductible and coinsurance arrangements There may be an overall maximum benefit payable on behalf of the client. These limits could be expressed in terms of the maximum benefit paid per year, or over the life of individual, or both. Limits can also apply to specific subsets of benefit (eg benefits in relation to mental or nervous issues). Policies that cover entire families may have a deductible that applies at family level rather than at individual level.

The original intent of major medical expense insurance was that the insurance policy would meet the major costs, but the insured would meet any more frequent lower costs. A version of major medical expense insurance was developed which covers more of the smaller expenses. This coverage is referred to as comprehensive major medical expense insurance.
This is more similar to the PMI product discussed in Section 1. However, it may still use coinsurance in order to reduce claims.

Various other versions of major medical expenses exist. For example there is a version of major medical expense cover with very high deductibles. This is intended to protect the policyholder against infrequent very large expenses.

Insurer's perspective Claims Major medical expense insurance is not a true indemnity product as the insured will normally retain a share of the cost of any claim above any deductible although it is still possible that the insurer will need to meet very large claim amounts. It may, therefore, be appropriate for the insurer to include limits within the terms of a policy to manage this, or consider appropriate reinsurance.
The deductible can be applied to reduce the likelihood of small claims being made.

External factors In some jurisdictions, regulation could have an impact on the product design and in terms of the benefits covered and also with respect to the proportion of any claim that the policy had to pay. This could potentially make it easier for consumers to compare products by partially standardising product coverage.
60-month moratorium (not 48); PED waiting period max 36 months; 1-hr/3-hr cashless TATs; 30-day claim settlement mandate; Bima Sugam digital marketplace launch. [src:Notes]

Changes in the regulatory or political landscape can potentially cause issues for the insurer. In particular once a change has taken place there will be a lag before any experience analysis can give an indication of the impact of the change. The insurer may also have difficulty in predicting consumer behaviour following the change.
14101 500m health and are our products

Flexibility Potentially the insurer could look to make their product more attractive to consumers by allowing greater flexibility in their product. This can enable the policyholder to choose the aspects of the product that are most attractive to them. This could be done by allowing the policyholder to vary the parameters of the product (eg deductibles, coinsurance, types of benefit covered). It could also potentially make the product more affordable to some customers (for example by increasing the deductible).

This approach can have implications though: Lapses greater product flexibility could generate more anti-selection, as consumers will tend to choose the coverage they are most likely to use. more complex benefit design could make it more difficult for the insurer to understand the product they have purchased.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

In the USA, there have been instances where some policyholders have purchased major medical expense products but only wished to have coverage for a short period of time. This has led to insurers seeing high short-term lapses, which potentially has an adverse impact on the profitability of this business.

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists. [src:Notes]
3.3 Meeting customer needs

Major medical expense insurance is potentially very attractive to a consumer where they can pay relatively small regular amounts to meet future medical expenses, rather than having to find potentially significant amounts of money if they incur large unexpected medical costs. This is particularly true for individuals living in jurisdictions where individual medical costs can be high, and where there may not be sufficient State- sponsored healthcare or where they do not have access to an employer-sponsored health plan. They can be particularly attractive to families, where the policy covers the medical costs of the family.
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

Note: Major medical expense insurance products will not normally fully indemnify the policyholder for all major medical costs as the policyholder will still be responsible for smaller claims due to the deductible (which can be significant - eg the deductible can be thousands of dollars in the USA), and possibly a proportion of claims in excess of the deductible. Even where the policyholder chooses a comprehensive version of major medical expense insurance there may be maximum limits on the product which means the policyholder could still be exposed to exceptionally large claim costs above the limit.

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists. [src:Notes]
Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918] [src:IAI_Synthesis]

It can be confusing for individuals in terms of understanding what major medical expense insurance actually covers. Where an insurer is required to have a minimum number of types of benefit coverage it is good *for* the policyholder as it prevents insurers from misleading policyholders, with products that have lesser benefits. It also prohibits insurers providing products with unexpected gaps in the benefit coverage.
